devotionals.title: A Brief History of Glory Presbyterian Church
devotionals.passageText: That He might present her to Himself a glorious church...
devotionals.article:
Glory Presbyterian Church was founded in 1881 and is recognised as the first Chinese Presbyterian church in Singapore. Here, "Chinese" refers especially to Chinese immigrants who came south from China to Singapore, rather than to Straits-born Chinese. Its history, however, can be traced back to 1856.

In 1856, Rev. Thomas McKenzie came to Singapore and worked with Elder Tan See Boo. Over the next four and a half years, he successfully established a mission among the Chinese before leaving for Victoria, Australia, in 1861.

In 1861, Rev. Benjamin Peach Keasberry of the London Missionary Society partnered Catechist Ching Kwang and Preacher Tan See Boo to establish a preaching point in Bukit Timah at Adam Road.

In 1862, land was granted by the government to build a church at the seventh-and-a-half milestone of Bukit Timah. Funds were raised by the Malay Chapel. The church was first known as the "Horse-Cart Road End Chapel" (马车路尾礼拜堂).

After Rev. Keasberry’s death in 1875, the congregation went through a period of uncertainty and was temporarily cared for by the Scottish Presbyterian Church, now Orchard Road Presbyterian Church. Our church then made a request to the Presbyterian Church in London for assistance.

In response, Rev. John Angus Bethune Cook and his wife were sent to serve the Chinese immigrant churches in Singapore. Before coming to Singapore, they first went to Swatow, China, to learn the Teochew language.

In 1881, Rev. Cook and his wife arrived in Singapore, and our congregation was formally established as the "Chinese Christian Church" (中华基督教会), with thirty-nine communicant members, mainly Teochew and Hokkien believers. Wang Chongzhen (王崇真) served as its first preacher.

The early chapel was an attap house. Around 1886, Zhang Heling (张鹤龄), a zealous Christian merchant from Foochow, initiated the building of a more permanent chapel. The foundation stone was laid in 1887. This chapel still stands today as part of the church premises and currently houses the English Service office.

Faith and education were closely linked in the church’s early ministry. Preacher Joseph Koh (许约瑟)founded the Horse-Cart Road End Free School (马车路尾义学) in 1889 and served as its first principal. In 1922, the school was renamed Pei Hwa School (培华学校).

In 1935, the revival meetings of Dr. John Sung（宋尚节） inspired many Christians in Singapore to evangelism. This spiritual awakening strengthened the church before the hardships of the Japanese Occupation. In 1943, Preacher Tang Poh Keng (董黼卿), who served as church evangelist, principal of Pei Hwa School, and secretary of the Synod, lost his life during the Japanese Sook Ching operation.

In 1947, the church adopted the name "Glory Church," based on Ephesians 5:27, which speaks of Christ presenting to Himself "a glorious church."

In 1956, the new sanctuary on the hill was dedicated to God; it remains the current sanctuary today.

Under the visionary leadership of Rev. Yap Kok Hu, the English service began in 1968 to shepherd the younger generation educated in English.

In the late 1990s and around the year 2000, the church went through a difficult period marked by differing views between several pastoral workers and the Session and Deacons’ Court. This eventually led to the departure of the pastoral workers and a group of young people from the church.

Yet even through this difficult season, the Lord continued to preserve His church.

The church also extended its witness through Glory Kindergarten (荣耀幼稚园), Glory Family Service Centre (荣耀家庭服务中心), and the establishment of what is now Yung Kwang Presbyterian Church (荣光堂). In 2013, the Chinese and English congregations were registered as one church.

From a small preaching station to the church, we know today, Glory Church’s history testifies to God’s faithful providence. Through every season, the Lord has preserved His people and entrusted the gospel to each generation. May Glory Church continue to glorify Him in faith, love, and holiness.

questions.text: Where do you see God’s faithfulness in Glory‘s history?
In 2026, our church announced that a church upgrading plan is in the pipeline. What is the significance of the two current buildings, built in 1887 and 1956, to Glory Church?
How does remembering our church history deepen our thanksgiving to God?

devotionals.passageText:
1 Paul and Timothy, servants of Christ Jesus,

To all the saints in Christ Jesus who are at Philippi, with the overseers and deacons:

2 Grace to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

questions.text: Context // Philippi was a wealthy gold producing Roman colony in Macedonia. They worshipped the Roman Emperors as well as Egyptian and other gods. Paul first visited them around AD 49 (See Acts 16:1-40) and established there the 1st church in Europe. Paul wrote this letter while in prison in Rome about 13 years later in AD 62

Observation // Who is writing to the Philippian Church? How are the authors described? (Is this surprising to you? Why?) Which 3 groups of people did they write to? What greeting does the author give to the church?

Meaning // The words "saints in Christ" means those who are holy and set apart by Jesus. Why do you think Paul calls the Philippian church by this title? How does this relate to his greeting of grace and peace after this? Where does grace and peace come from?

Application // From God and Jesus comes grace (undeserved favor) and the peace of God (4:7). As Paul reminds the Phlippian church of this, let us too be reminded and think about when in our lives have we experienced this grace and peace. Where will you need God's grace and peace this week? Take some time to praise God for His grace and peace.

devotionals.passageText: 3 I thank my God in all my remembrance of you, 4 always in every prayer of mine for you all making my prayer with joy, 5 because of your partnership in the gospel from the first day until now. 6 And I am sure of this, that he who began a good work in you will bring it to completion at the day of Jesus Christ.

questions.text: Context // What do you remember from the passage yesterday?
Observation // What does Paul thank God in prayer for? How is he feeling as he prays? Why? Who produces this partnership and good work?
Meaning // What do you learn about God's work in the gospel (in the past, present and future)? What gives Paul joy and confidence as he prays for the Philippian church?

Application // God is working in our lives. Where have you seen God's good work in you this past year - even if incomplete? In the last year, which believer have you seen God do His good work in? What does that good work look like (it could be salvation, transformation in character, fleeing sin, sharing the gospel)? Take some time to pray for him/her with joy as we see God do His good work.

devotionals.passageText: 7 It is right for me to feel this way about you all, because I hold you in my heart, for you are all partakers with me of grace, both in my imprisonment and in the defense and confirmation of the gospel. 8 For God is my witness, how I yearn for you all with the affection of Christ Jesus.

questions.text: Context // What do you remember from the passage yesterday? How does Paul feel?
Observation // Why does Paul feel this way? What does he say about the Philippian Christians? What did they do for him? How does Paul describe his affection?
Meaning // What do you learn about "partnership in the gospel" v5? How does knowing that Paul is writing this letter from prison help us understand this more?

Application // Partnership in the gospel means joint-ownership — sharing both in spreading the gospel (furtherance) and strengthening others in their faith (confirmation). Paul lived this out even from prison, sustained by others who partnered with him in his hardship. This week, who could you partner with in gospel work — whether by sharing Christ with someone, helping a believer grow, or standing with someone in their hardship? Who is one person you could reach out to?

devotionals.passageText: 9 And it is my prayer that your love may abound more and more, with knowledge and all discernment, 10 so that you may approve what is excellent, and so be pure and blameless for the day of Christ, 11 filled with the fruit of righteousness that comes through Jesus Christ, to the glory and praise of God.

questions.text: Context // What do you remember from the passage yesterday? What was Paul thankful for and confident about regarding the Philippians?

Observation // What does Paul pray for the Philippians? What three things does he pray their love would grow in? What does he pray they will be able to do as a result? What is Paul's ultimate goal for them?

Meaning // Why does Paul pray for love to abound specifically "with knowledge and discernment" rather than just more love? How are love, knowledge, and discernment connected? What does it mean to be "filled with the fruit of righteousness" and where does this fruit come from? Why does Paul say this all leads to "the glory and praise of God"?

Application // Paul doesn't just pray that the Philippians would love more, but that their love would grow in wisdom, able to test what truly matters and choose it. Think of a decision or relationship in your life right now where love needs to be paired with discernment, not just good intentions. Ask God this week to grow your love in knowledge and discernment so that your life would be marked by the fruit of righteousness, for His glory — not your own.
Context // Saturday questions
Observation //
Meaning //
Application //

devotionals.passageText: 3 I thank my God in all my remembrance of you, 4 always in every prayer of mine for you all making my prayer with joy, 5 because of your partnership in the gospel from the first day until now. 6 And I am sure of this, that he who began a good work in you will bring it to completion at the day of Jesus Christ. 7 It is right for me to feel this way about you all, because I hold you in my heart, for you are all partakers with me of grace, both in my imprisonment and in the defense and confirmation of the gospel. 8 For God is my witness, how I yearn for you all with the affection of Christ Jesus.

questions.text: Context //
Observation //
Meaning //
Application // Write down your application and prayer
Context //
Observation //
Meaning //
Application //

Context // Matthew 24:3 tells us that Jesus was teaching his disciples about the end times. Today's passage is the 3rd of a series of 3 parables. What does Jesus say about the end times in the 1st two parables (v36-44 & v45-51)?

Observation // What did the 10 virgins do in the parable? How is the bridegroom described? How are the wise and foolish virgins contrasted (actions and consequences)? What was the warning given in v13?
Meaning // Read Matt 7:23, what is similar to our passage today? Read Matt 9:14-15, who does the bridegroom represent? What do you learn about the return of Jesus?

Application // The foolish virgins expected to meet the groom but were unprepared when he was delayed. Are you prepared for Jesus coming? Why or why not? (What does it mean to be prepared? We will see more in tomorrow's passage.)
Context // Yesterday's parable tells us to be prepared for Jesus' coming. How does today's parable add to that lesson?

Observation // What does the master do? How are those with 5, 2 and 1 talent described? What is similar and what is different? What does the master tell each one when he returned? What is the warning given in 29-30?
Meaning // How does the master want his servants to act in his absence / in awaiting his return? How does today's passage teach us to be prepared for Jesus' coming?

Application // The one-talent servant buried his gift out of fear, doing nothing with what he was given. Is there a gift, opportunity, or responsibility God has entrusted to you that you've been sitting on — out of fear, comparison to others, or thinking it's "too small" to matter? What would it look like to put it to use this week, trusting God with the outcome?
Context // What to you remember about our passage from yesterday? How is it related to our passage today?
Observation // How is the return of Jesus described? What does He do on that day? What do the sheep do and what is the consequence for them?
Meaning // What do you learn about God / Jesus? How does this passage help us to be prepared for Jesus' return?

Application // The sheep didn't serve "the least of these" in order to earn a reward — their care for others simply flowed out of who they were as Jesus's people. Is there someone overlooked, in need, or easy to pass by that God is putting in front of you right now (the hungry, the stranger, the sick, the imprisoned)? What would it look like to serve them this week, not to prove your faith, but as an overflow of it?
Context // Today's passage is a continuation of yesterday's. What did you learn about those who were sheep / on the right?

Observation // What does Jesus say to the goats? What is their consequence? How do the goats respond to Jesus's words? How are the goats' actions (or lack of actions) different from the sheep's in yesterday's passage?

Meaning // Why are the goats surprised by Jesus's words in v44? What does this reveal about how they viewed their own relationship with Him? What do you learn about the reality and finality of judgment from this passage?

Application // The goats weren't condemned for what they did, but for what they failed to do — and they seemed unaware that their neglect of others was neglect of Jesus Himself. Is there a blind spot in your life where you might be overlooking someone in need without realizing it's Jesus you're overlooking? Ask God this week to open your eyes to those around you, and to move you from awareness to action.
Context // Saturday questions
Observation //
Meaning //
Application //
Context // Yesterday's passage reminded us of being faithful in the talents and gifts God has given to us as we await Jesus' return. It links to actions and rewards that God promises

Observation // Jesus' return will be in glory with all the angels with him, and He will sit on His glorious throne v31. He will gather the nations and separate them. 
The sheep are those who in v35-36 fed the brothers who were hungry, and thirsty, welcomed the stranger, clothed the naked, visited the sick and those in prison. Their reward was blessing by the Father, and will inherit the kingdom v34
Meaning // Jesus will return and He will judge.
Application // Write down your application and prayer
Context //
Observation //
Meaning //
Application //

Context // In Paul's 3rd missionary journey, he went to Ephesus (Acts 19). Being near a habour, it was a centre of trade and the captital city of the Roman province of Asia. He wrote this letter to the Ephesians around AD 62 while in prison in Rome.
Observation // What does Paul say God has blessed us with (v3)? List out each spiritual blessing Paul mentions in verses 4-14 (eg v4 chose). Who carries out each of these actions?

Meaning // What do you learn about the role of the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit in God's plan to bless us with all spiritual blessing? Why do you think Paul repeats the phrase "to the praise of his glory" (v6, 12, 14)?

Application // Paul overwhelms the Ephesian church by beginning his letter wtih all that God has done for us in Christ. Take time to slowly reread verses 3-14 and identify one blessing that stands out to you personally right now. Spend time in prayer thanking God specifically for that blessing, "to the praise of his glory."
Context // What do you remember from yesterday's reading? How does our passage today continue its theme and thoughts?

Observation // What has Paul heard about the Ephesians that prompts his thanksgiving (v15)? What does Paul pray for them (v17-19)? List out what he prays they would know. What does God do for Christ in v20-23?
Meaning // What do you learn about God and His power? About Jesus? About God's church?

Application // Christ's supremacy over the universe is God's gift to the church — He fills it as His body, so it can display Him to the world (v22-23). Where do you need to rely less on your own strength, and more on Christ actively filling and empowering His body through you this week? Take time to pray for our church, trusting Christ to sustain it in any struggle or weakness we face.
Context // Paul in Ephesians 2 describes the church as one new body of Christ, made up of Jews and Gentiles, reconcilled to God and each other through Jesus' work on the cross.
Observation // What does Paul say was given to him (v2-3)? What is "the mystery of Christ" that Paul refers to in v4,6? How was this mystery revealed, and to whom?
Meaning // What do you learn about God's plan for His church? Why is it significant that the Gentiles are described as "heirs together with Israel"?

Application // God's plan all along was for people from every nation to become one new body in Christ, full heirs together with no one excluded. Who is a person or people group you're aware of that hasn't yet heard this good news? Take time this week to pray for them by name, and consider what step you could take to be part of God revealing this mystery to them: whether through prayer, giving, going, or simply sharing the gospel with someone near you.
Context // What do you remember from our passage yesterday? How does today's passage build on the idea of the "mystery of Christ" v4?
Observation // What does Paul say he became, and how (v7)? What was he called to do? Who is meant to learn God's wisdom through the church, and how (v10-12)?
Meaning // What do you learn about God's plans and purposes? What do you learn about the church?

Application // God's plan for making His wisdom known is through the church, not despite it. How does knowing this change the way you view your church's role in missions and evangelism? Is there a way you can actively participate in the church's mission to make Christ known (eg by praying for missionaries, giving, going, or sharing the gospel with someone this week)?
Context // Saturday questions
Observation //
Meaning //
Application //
Context //
Observation //
Meaning //
Application // Write down your application and prayer
Context //
Observation //
Meaning //
Application //
Context // Our passage today follows Jesus' transfiguration on the mountain (9:28-36). How does today's passage contrast with what they just experienced?
Observation // What does the man in the crowd ask Jesus to do? How does Jesus respond? What does Jesus tell his disciples right after this? What was their response?

Meaning // Why does Jesus choose this exact moment to speak about His coming suffering? What does this contrast reveal about what Jesus's mission actually looks like? What do you learn about his disciples?

Application // Right after a miracle that amazed the crowd, Jesus points His disciples to the cross. Where have you expected following Jesus to look easy or impressive, only to find it harder than you thought? Bring that honestly to Jesus and ask Him to help you follow Him even when it doesn't look like what you expected.
Context // What do you remember from yesterday's passage? What insight does it give to our reading today?
Observation // What did the disciples argue about? How does Jesus respond to their argument? What does John report to Jesus in v49? How does Jesus respond to John (v50)?
Meaning // What do you learn about the disciples and what they value? How does Jesus view greatness and belonging?

Application // While Jesus was preparing to suffer and serve, His disciples were consumed with who was greatest, and who was in or out. Where have you been seeking recognition instead of serving others? Is there someone you've dismissed for not being "one of us"? Ask Jesus to reshape how you see greatness and belonging this week.
Context // What do you remember from yesterday's passage? What does it mean that Jesus "set his face to go to Jerusalem" (v51)?
Observation // Who did Jesus send ahead, and why (v52)? How did the Samaritan village respond to Jesus? What did James and John ask Jesus to do? How does Jesus respond to them?
Meaning // What do you learn about James and John from their reaction to rejection by the Samaritans? How is Jesus different?

Application // Jesus was focused on going to the cross to save people, while His disciples wanted to call down judgment on those who rejected Him. Where have you responded to rejection with a desire for people to "get what they deserve," instead of desiring their salvation? Ask Jesus for His same heart this week.
Context // What do you remember from yesterday's passage? How does it link to our passage today?
Observation // How does Jesus interact with these 3 men along the road?
Meaning // What do you learn about people's expectations about following Jesus? What do you learn about Jesus' priorities?

Application // Jesus's call takes first place over everything (comfort, family, etc) and once we commit, there's no looking back (v62). Where have you expected following Jesus to fit comfortably into your plans, or found yourself glancing back at what you left behind? Ask Jesus this week to help you put Him first, fully, without turning back.

devotionals.title:
No Turning Back
(written by Bro. Lawrence Tong)
devotionals.passageText: 57 As they were going along the road, someone said to him, “I will follow you wherever you go.” 58 And Jesus said to him, “Foxes have holes, and birds of the air have nests, but the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head.” 59 To another he said, “Follow me.” But he said, “Lord, let me first go and bury my father.” 60 And Jesus said to him, “Leave the dead to bury their own dead. But as for you, go and proclaim the kingdom of God.” 61 Yet another said, “I will follow you, Lord, but let me first say farewell to those at my home.” 62 Jesus said to him, “No one who puts his hand to the plow and looks back is fit for the kingdom of God.”
devotionals.article:
There are moments in life when a single decision changes everything.

In Luke 9, Jesus encounters three people who express a desire to follow Him.

Yet each faces a choice. Would they allow competing priorities, personal attachments, or future uncertainties to stand in the way of wholehearted discipleship?

Jesus concludes with these challenging words:

“No one who puts a hand to the plow and looks back is fit for service in the kingdom of God.”

At first glance, His words seem demanding. Yet behind them is an invitation—-to a life centered on Him.

Nearly forty-eight years ago, I said yes to following Jesus through missionary service with Operation Mobilization. There were seasons of financial pressure, difficult decisions, and uncertain paths ahead. Yet if I were given the opportunity to begin again, I would make the same choice.

Why?

Because I have discovered that Jesus is worthy.

The world constantly invites us to look back—to what we might have achieved, accumulated, or experienced had we chosen a different path. Jesus invites us to look forward. He calls us to trust Him and believe that no sacrifice made for His sake is ever wasted.

Whether Jesus leads us across the street or across the world, His invitation remains the same: “Follow Me.” When we fix our eyes on Him, we discover that the greatest joy in life is not found in what we leave behind, but in the One we follow.

For those who choose to follow Him, there is no better way to live—and no reason to turn back.

questions.text: What might Jesus be asking you to trust Him with today?
Is there anything causing you to look back rather than move forward in faith?
What would wholehearted discipleship look like for you in this season of life?
